title: Using 1x1 Convs to Reduce Dimensionality
concept: Because a 1x1 convolution can output fewer channels than it takes in, it's a cheap way to shrink channel depth before a more expensive larger-kernel convolution runs.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A 1x1 convolution mixes information across...

options.text: Channels, at a single fixed pixel position
Neighboring pixels, within a single channel
Nothing — it passes data through completely unchanged

exercises.explain: Right — channel mixing, spatial grid untouched. Now let's use that mixing to solve a real cost problem.

cards.title: Deep networks get expensive fast
cards.body: As a CNN goes deeper, channel counts balloon — 64, then 128, then 256, then 512. Running a 3x3 convolution over 256 input channels means every single output value needs a weighted sum over 3×3×256 numbers. That cost multiplies with every filter. What if you could shrink the channel count first, before paying that price?

cards.title: Dimensionality reduction with a 1x1 conv
cards.body: A 1x1 convolution can output fewer channels than it takes in. Feed in 256 channels, use just 64 1x1 filters, and the output has only 64 channels — same height and width, compressed depth. This is called dimensionality reduction, and it's cheap because a 1x1 conv's cost scales with channels, not with a 3x3 spatial footprint.

cards.title: A cheat-sheet before the deep dive
cards.body: Imagine a 256-recipe cookbook. Before painstakingly cooking (the expensive 3x3 work), you distill it down to a 64-recipe cheat-sheet that captures the essentials. Cooking from the smaller cheat-sheet is faster — you're doing the expensive work on a smaller set, not the full binder.

cards.title: Counting the savings
cards.body: A direct 3x3 conv on 256 channels → 256 channels costs about 3×3×256×256 = 589,824 multiply-adds per pixel. Squeeze first with a 1x1 conv (256→64): 1×1×256×64 = 16,384. Then run the 3x3 on the smaller 64 channels (64→256): 3×3×64×256 = 147,456. Total: 16,384 + 147,456 = 163,840 — barely a third of the direct cost.

cards.title: Reduction isn't random deletion
cards.body: Shrinking 256 channels down to 64 can sound like throwing away 192 channels' worth of information at random. It isn't — the 64 output channels are learned weighted combinations of all 256 inputs, trained to keep whatever the network finds most useful, not an arbitrary chop.

exercises.prompt: Why does squeezing channels with a 1x1 conv before a 3x3 conv save computation?

options.text: Because the expensive 3x3 conv now runs on far fewer channels than the original input
Because 1x1 convs are always run on a faster chip than 3x3 convs
Because squeezing shrinks the image's height and width, so there are fewer pixels

exercises.explain: Correct — the costly 3x3 spatial work only has to process the smaller, squeezed channel count.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a 1x1 conv that reduces 256 channels down to 64 discards the extra 192 channels completely at random.
exercises.explain: False — the 64 output channels are learned weighted combinations of all 256 input channels, not a random discard.

exercises.sentence: Shrinking channel depth with a 1x1 convolution before an expensive 3x3 convolution is called dimensionality ___.
exercises.explain: Right — this is dimensionality reduction, and it's why 1x1 convs are so useful for cutting compute cost.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes 1x1-conv dimensionality reduction?

options.text: A 1x1 conv with fewer output filters than input channels compresses channel depth cheaply before costlier layers run
A pooling layer that halves the image's height and width
A technique for converting a color image into grayscale

exercises.explain: Exactly — fewer output filters than input channels means a cheap, learned channel-depth squeeze.

exercises.prompt: A layer receives a feature map with 512 channels. It applies a 1x1 conv with 128 filters to reduce channel depth before a 3x3 conv runs. How many channels does the feature map have immediately after that 1x1 conv?
exercises.explain: Correct — a 1x1 conv's output channel count equals its number of filters, so 128 filters means 128 channels, ready for a cheaper 3x3 pass. Next: what happens after that 3x3 work is done.
